narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dreams despite the challenges many face in academic pathways like this. Your profile includes a strong start with an 8th decile ranking in your first-year grades—well above the typical student's 6th decile—alongside a solid undergraduate GPA of 2.90, which is close to the average of 3.21, and plans to attend full-time like most applicants. Your family falls into the middle income bracket, which is common in the applicant pool. However, your LSAT score of 21.50 stands out as notably lower than the average of 36.70, and your third-year decile dropped to 4th, below the typical 6th decile. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated your probability of failure at 45%. This prediction tipped the scale toward rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants with the strongest forecasted chances of bar success to maintain high pass rates. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, which had the strongest pull in that direction compared to other elements in your profile. Counterbalancing this somewhat were your impressive first-year decile and undergraduate GPA, which were the next biggest influences nudging the prediction toward bar passage, showing your early potential and overall academic steadiness. Your third-year decile had a smaller opposing effect, slightly favoring failure, while your full-time status and family income played minor supportive roles toward success. Compared to typical students who get approved, your LSAT is a key area where you differ most, as higher scores there strongly correlate with bar success in the dataset. While this decision is disappointing, it doesn't define your capabilities—many overcome similar hurdles. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year performance through targeted study, and reapplying with an updated profile that highlights your resilience as a Black woman in law. We're rooting for your success.
